title: American Philosophical Society

infobox.value: (**APS**) is an American scholarly organization and [learned society](/wiki/Learned_society "Learned society") founded in 1743 in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") that promotes knowledge in the [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities") and [natural sciences](/wiki/Natural_sciences "Natural sciences") through research, professional meetings, publications, [library resources](/wiki/Source_text "Source text"), and community outreach. It was founded by the polymath [Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin "Benjamin Franklin") and is considered the first learned society founded in what became the United States.

in 1743 by [Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin "Benjamin Franklin"), [James Alexander](/wiki/James_Alexander_(lawyer) "James Alexander (lawyer)"), [Francis Hopkinson](/wiki/Francis_Hopkinson "Francis Hopkinson"), [John Bartram](/wiki/John_Bartram "John Bartram"), [Philip Syng Jr.](/wiki/Philip_Syng "Philip Syng"), and others[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) as an offshoot of an earlier club, the [Junto](/wiki/Junto_(club) "Junto (club)").

The **American Philosophical Society** (**APS**) is an American scholarly organization and [learned society](/wiki/Learned_society "Learned society") founded in 1743 in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") that promotes knowledge in the [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities") and [natural sciences](/wiki/Natural_sciences "Natural sciences") through research, professional meetings, publications, [library resources](/wiki/Source_text "Source text"), and community outreach. It was founded by the polymath [Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin "Benjamin Franklin") and is considered the first learned society founded in what became the United States.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Nullo_Discrimine_The_American_Philosophical_Society.png/250px-Nullo_Discrimine_The_American_Philosophical_Society.png)](/wiki/File:Nullo_Discrimine_The_American_Philosophical_Society.png)

The society's seal

[Philosophical Hall](/wiki/Philosophical_Hall "Philosophical Hall"), the society's headquarters and a museum, is located just east of [Independence Hall](/wiki/Independence_Hall "Independence Hall") in [Independence National Historical Park](/wiki/Independence_National_Historical_Park "Independence National Historical Park"). In 1965, in recognition of the building's history, it was designated a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark").

The society has about 1,000 elected members. As of April 2020, 5,710 members had been inducted since its creation. Through research grants, published journals, the American Philosophical Society Museum, an extensive library, and regular meetings, the society supports a variety of disciplines in the humanities and the sciences.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Philosophical_Society&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Feke_-_Benjamin_Franklin.png/250px-Feke_-_Benjamin_Franklin.png)](/wiki/File:Feke_-_Benjamin_Franklin.png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Franklin_Hall_meeting_preparation.jpg/250px-Franklin_Hall_meeting_preparation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Franklin_Hall_meeting_preparation.jpg)

The American Philosophical Society was founded as the **Philosophical Society** in 1743 by [Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin "Benjamin Franklin"), [James Alexander](/wiki/James_Alexander_(lawyer) "James Alexander (lawyer)"), [Francis Hopkinson](/wiki/Francis_Hopkinson "Francis Hopkinson"), [John Bartram](/wiki/John_Bartram "John Bartram"), [Philip Syng Jr.](/wiki/Philip_Syng "Philip Syng"), and others[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) as an offshoot of an earlier club, the [Junto](/wiki/Junto_(club) "Junto (club)").

Early members included: [Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin "Benjamin Franklin"), [John Dickinson](/wiki/John_Dickinson "John Dickinson"), [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington"), [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams"), [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson"),[[4]](#cite_note-AAA-4) [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton"), [James McHenry](/wiki/James_McHenry "James McHenry"), [Thomas Paine](/wiki/Thomas_Paine "Thomas Paine"), [David Rittenhouse](/wiki/David_Rittenhouse "David Rittenhouse"), [Peter Stephen Du Ponceau](/wiki/Peter_Stephen_Du_Ponceau "Peter Stephen Du Ponceau"), [Nicholas Biddle](/wiki/Nicholas_Biddle_(banker) "Nicholas Biddle (banker)"), [Owen Biddle](/wiki/Owen_Biddle_Sr. "Owen Biddle Sr."), [Benjamin Rush](/wiki/Benjamin_Rush "Benjamin Rush"), [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison"), [Michael Hillegas](/wiki/Michael_Hillegas "Michael Hillegas"), [John Marshall](/wiki/John_Marshall "John Marshall"), [Charles Pettit](/wiki/Charles_Pettit "Charles Pettit"), and [John Andrews](/wiki/John_Andrews_(clergyman) "John Andrews (clergyman)").

It was common at the time for intellectual societies to invite members from around the world, where the society recruited members from other countries, including [Alexander von Humboldt](/wiki/Alexander_von_Humboldt "Alexander von Humboldt"), the [Marquis de Lafayette](/wiki/Marquis_de_Lafayette "Marquis de Lafayette"), [Baron von Steuben](/wiki/Baron_von_Steuben "Baron von Steuben"), [Tadeusz Kościuszko](/wiki/Tadeusz_Ko%C5%9Bciuszko "Tadeusz Kościuszko"), and [Princess Dashkova](/wiki/Princess_Dashkova "Princess Dashkova"). The society lapsed into inactivity by 1746, but was revived in 1767.

On January 2, 1769, the society united with the American Society for Promoting Useful Knowledge under the name **American Philosophical Society Held at Philadelphia for Promoting Useful Knowledge**. Franklin was elected the first president.[[5]](#cite_note-5) During this time, the society maintained a standing Committee on American Improvements; one of its investigations was to study the prospects for a canal to connect the [Chesapeake Bay](/wiki/Chesapeake_Bay "Chesapeake Bay") and the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River").[[6]](#cite_note-6) The [Chesapeake and Delaware Canal](/wiki/Chesapeake_and_Delaware_Canal "Chesapeake and Delaware Canal"), which had been proposed by Thomas Gilpin, Sr., was built in the 1820s.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Following the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), the society looked for leadership to [Francis Hopkinson](/wiki/Francis_Hopkinson "Francis Hopkinson"), one of the signatories of the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"). Under his influence, the society received land from the government of Pennsylvania, along with a plot of land in Philadelphia, where Philosophical Hall now stands.

[Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin"), [Robert Frost](/wiki/Robert_Frost "Robert Frost"), [Louis Pasteur](/wiki/Louis_Pasteur "Louis Pasteur"), [Elizabeth Cabot Agassiz](/wiki/Elizabeth_Cabot_Agassiz "Elizabeth Cabot Agassiz"), [John James Audubon](/wiki/John_James_Audubon "John James Audubon"), [Linus Pauling](/wiki/Linus_Pauling "Linus Pauling"), [Margaret Mead](/wiki/Margaret_Mead "Margaret Mead"), [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson"), [Maria Mitchell](/wiki/Maria_Mitchell "Maria Mitchell"), and [Thomas Edison](/wiki/Thomas_Edison "Thomas Edison") were all prominent members of the society.

Many members of the [Society of the Cincinnati](/wiki/Society_of_the_Cincinnati "Society of the Cincinnati") were among the APS's first board members and contributors; the APS and SOC still maintain an informal, collegial relationship.

## Membership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Philosophical_Society&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Membership")]

Membership of the APS "honors extraordinary accomplishments in all fields." It has about 1,000 elected members, comprising about 840 "resident" members (United States citizens or those working or living in the United States) and about 160 "international" members. As of February 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Philosophical_Society&action=edit) it had elected 5,890 members since its foundation.[[8]](#cite_note-elected-8)

Over that history, 220 members have been from [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"), 117 from [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University"), 91 from [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University"), and 86 from the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"). Ten academic institutions have each been affiliated with 50 or more members:[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Philosophical_Society&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Awards")]

In 1786, the society established the [Magellanic Premium](/wiki/Magellanic_Premium "Magellanic Premium"), a prize for achievement in "navigation, [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"), or natural philosophy," the oldest scientific prize awarded by an American institution, which it still awards.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Other awards include the Barzun Prize for [cultural history](/wiki/Cultural_history "Cultural history"), the Judson Daland Prize for Outstanding Achievement in Clinical Investigation, the [Benjamin Franklin Medal](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin_Medal_(American_Philosophical_Society) "Benjamin Franklin Medal (American Philosophical Society)") for distinguished achievement in the sciences, the [Lashley Award](/wiki/Lashley_Award "Lashley Award") for [neurobiology](/wiki/Neurobiology "Neurobiology"), the Lewis Award for the best book published by the society in the year, and the [Thomas Jefferson Medal](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson_Medal_for_Distinguished_Achievement_in_the_Arts,_Humanities,_and_Social_Sciences "Thomas Jefferson Medal for Distinguished Achievement in the Arts, Humanities, and Social Sciences") for distinguished achievement in the arts, humanities, or social sciences.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Publications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Philosophical_Society&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Publications")]

The society has published the *Transactions of the American Philosophical Society* since 1771. Five issues appear each year. The *[Proceedings](/wiki/Proceedings_of_the_American_Philosophical_Society "Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society")* have appeared since 1838; they publish the papers delivered at the society's biannual meetings. The society has also published *[The Papers of Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/The_Papers_of_Benjamin_Franklin "The Papers of Benjamin Franklin")*, [Joseph Henry](/wiki/Joseph_Henry "Joseph Henry"), [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn"), and [Meriwether Lewis](/wiki/Meriwether_Lewis "Meriwether Lewis"), and [William Clark](/wiki/William_Clark "William Clark"). [Jane Aitken](/wiki/Jane_Aitken "Jane Aitken") bound 400 volumes for the society.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames197126-12)

The society also has an expansive archive on framer of the [U.S. constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States") [John Dickinson](/wiki/John_Dickinson "John Dickinson").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

APS holds the ACLS Collection (American Council of Learned Societies Committee on Native American Languages, American Philosophical Society) which is a collection of indigenous language documents from around the United States including recordings of the [Odawa language](/wiki/Ottawa_dialect "Ottawa dialect") from northern Michigan.[[14]](#cite_note-14) APS has created a guide[[15]](#cite_note-15) to help provide broad coverage of the Native American and Indigenous archival collections at the Library & Museum of the American Philosophical Society. These materials date from 1553 to 2020 and include manuscript, audio, and visual materials relating to Indigenous peoples throughout the Americas.

The society also has a collection of manuscripts on the history of the British colonies, Revolutionary War, the history of American science, quantum physics, Charles Darwin and evolution, genetics and the history of technology.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Philosophical Hall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Philosophical_Society&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Philosophical Hall")]

Main article: [Philosophical Hall](/wiki/Philosophical_Hall "Philosophical Hall")

[Philosophical Hall](/wiki/Philosophical_Hall "Philosophical Hall"), at 104 South Fifth Street, Philadelphia, between [Chestnut](/wiki/Chestnut_Street_(Philadelphia) "Chestnut Street (Philadelphia)") and [Walnut](/wiki/Walnut_Street_(Philadelphia) "Walnut Street (Philadelphia)") Streets, immediately south of [Old City Hall](/wiki/Old_City_Hall_(Philadelphia) "Old City Hall (Philadelphia)"), was built in 1785–1789 to house the society and designed by Samuel Vaughan in the [Federal style](/wiki/Federal_architecture "Federal architecture").[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-philarchmit-18) A third floor was added in 1890 to accommodate the expanding library, but was removed in 1948–1950,[[18]](#cite_note-philarchmit-18) when the building was restored to its original appearance for the creation of [Independence National Historical Park](/wiki/Independence_National_Historical_Park "Independence National Historical Park").[[19]](#cite_note-19) In 2001, it was opened to the public as The American Philosophical Society Museum, hosting revolving, thematic exhibitions that explore intersections of history, art, and science. The museum features works of art, scientific instruments, original manuscripts, rare books, natural history specimens, and curiosities of all kinds from the APS's own collections, along with objects on loan from other institutions.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Library Hall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Philosophical_Society&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Library Hall")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/American_Philosophical_Society_Library_Hall_reading_room.jpg/250px-American_Philosophical_Society_Library_Hall_reading_room.jpg)](/wiki/File:American_Philosophical_Society_Library_Hall_reading_room.jpg)

Reading room for researchers at [Library Hall](/wiki/Library_Company_of_Philadelphia "Library Company of Philadelphia") in 2019

In 1789–90, the [Library Company of Philadelphia](/wiki/Library_Company_of_Philadelphia "Library Company of Philadelphia") (LCP), now located at 1314 [Locust Street](/wiki/Locust_Street "Locust Street") in Philadelphia, built its headquarters directly across 5th Street from APS. In 1884, LCP sold its building, which was demolished for the expansion of the Drexel & Company Building in 1887. This building was demolished in the mid-1950s, during the creation of Independence National Historical Park.

APS built a library on the site in 1958 and recreated the façade of the old LCP building.

### Benjamin Franklin Hall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Philosophical_Society&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Benjamin Franklin Hall")]

APS restored the former Farmers' & Mechanics' Bank building at 425–29 [Chestnut Street](/wiki/Chestnut_Street_(Philadelphia) "Chestnut Street (Philadelphia)"), which was built in 1854–55 to the design of John M. Gries in the [Italianate style](/wiki/Italianate_architecture "Italianate architecture"),[[21]](#cite_note-21) to serve as a lecture hall. It is the site of meetings and most major events the society hosts.[[22]](#cite_note-apsdir-22)

### Richardson Hall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Philosophical_Society&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Richardson Hall")]

Constance C. and Edgar P. Richardson Hall at 431 [Chestnut Street](/wiki/Chestnut_Street_(Philadelphia) "Chestnut Street (Philadelphia)"), immediately west of Benjamin Franklin Hall, is the former Pennsylvania Company for Insurances on Lives and Granting Annuities Building, which was built between 1871 and 1873 and designed by [Addison Hutton](/wiki/Addison_Hutton "Addison Hutton").[[23]](#cite_note-23) It contains offices and the Consortium for History of Science, Technology and Medicine.[[22]](#cite_note-apsdir-22)
